topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor traced the worn grooves of the mahogany table, the scent of linseed oil suddenly sharp in the air. For forty years, woodworking had been her sanctuary—a quiet, precise world where she commanded the grain and shaped solid things with practiced, gentle hands. Now, her son, Leo, was a whirlwind of tiny, insistent demands. He didn't just want to look at her tools; he wanted to *use* them. Last week, he’d managed to wedge a miniature, perfectly carved bird—a piece she had spent three days perfecting—into the mechanism of her antique lathe, jamming the whole thing with a grating screech. When she tried to calmly explain the delicate balance, Leo had just stared up at her, his eyes shining with a fierce, unyielding focus that made her throat tighten. She retreated to her workshop, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in her chest. Every time she picked up a chisel, she felt a distinct, sickening lurch in her stomach, a feeling of being utterly out of her depth, a master craftsman reduced to an overwhelmed caretaker for a tiny, demanding apprentice.

[story 2]

"Just let me try, Mom," Leo pleaded, his voice high and earnest, his small fingers gripping the edge of her workbench. He wasn't interested in sanding; he was obsessed with the intricate detailing of the miniature ship models Eleanor used to build for pleasure. He’d somehow managed to acquire dozens of tiny, specialized clamps and miniature sandpaper blocks, all commandeered from her supply cabinet. When she tried to gently redirect him toward a simple pine board, he’d simply wave a dismissive hand, his attention already fixed on a half-finished galleon, demanding she show him the 'real' techniques. Watching him meticulously glue minuscule flags onto his wooden vessels, his brow furrowed in intense concentration, Eleanor felt a profound sense of inadequacy wash over her. Her own creations, once sources of deep satisfaction, now felt clumsy and large in comparison to the miniature, flawless worlds Leo was conjuring. She kept wiping down the bench, her movements stiff, the metallic tang of the wood dust a bitter reminder that her expertise was being eclipsed by his sudden, consuming passion.

[story 3]

I remember the afternoon vividly. I was attempting to restore an old wooden cabinet, a project that usually brought a quiet, satisfying rhythm to my days. But today, the rhythm was shattered. My twelve-year-old, Finn, had descended upon the room like a tiny, determined storm. He wasn't interested in the cabinet; he was interested in the precision tools scattered across the floor—the files, the rasps, the tiny calipers. He wasn't making furniture; he was meticulously attempting to replicate the microscopic joints of a clockwork mechanism he’d seen in a documentary. When I tried to gently explain that those tools were for shaping wood, he just shook his head, his expression one of profound disappointment that I couldn't meet his level of detailed understanding. He looked at me as if I were speaking a foreign language, my attempts at instruction falling flat against the sheer intensity of his fixation. I stood back, my hands trembling slightly as I watched him painstakingly try to align two minuscule pieces of brass. A heavy weight settled on my shoulders; I felt exposed, as if my lifelong skill had become nothing more than an obstacle in his pursuit of something infinitely more compelling.
